content.assessment: This proposal is definitionally the demographic counterfactual and is the best-supported option under this lens by construction, since it is derived directly from the strongest-rated demographic findings (accelerating cohort decline, structurally embedded 20+ year trajectory, statutory class-size mechanics, and regional contraction). It correctly identifies that passive contraction will not be geographically uniform: urban flagship schools with path-dependent prestige may sustain oversubscription even as rural provision mechanically collapses under statutory minimum-class-size rules, producing an outcome that reduces aggregate selective-track share while potentially worsening geographic and, by extension, SES-linked inequities, given the within-locality SES-competency divergence documented in areas with accessible tracks (contested against the international PISA-based finding of no significant SES-correlation change post-reform). As a pure counterfactual, it clarifies what any active proposal must outperform, but by design it takes no position on the composition or distributional consequences of the demographic force it describes.
opportunities: Serves as an empirically disciplined benchmark against which the demographic assumptions embedded in all four active proposals (t1-t4) can be tested, particularly regarding capacity and rural viability timelines
strengths: Directly grounded in the strongest-rated evidence in the record: record-low and still-declining birth cohorts, a structurally embedded 20+ year decline mechanism, and statutory class-size thresholds that mechanically force contraction independent of policy choice

Correctly anticipates that contraction will be spatially uneven, with rural provision facing existential viability pressure while urban/Budapest flagship tracks may retain prestige-driven oversubscription, a pattern consistent with existing geographic concentration data
threats: Uneven passive contraction could allow church/private schools outside state demographic-consolidation pressure to gain relative share, altering sector composition in ways beyond public planning intent, and could entrench rather than resolve the SES-linked outcome divergence found in localities with accessible early tracks (contested by the international PISA-based counter-finding)
weaknesses: As a no-new-policy baseline, it offers no mechanism to correct the SES/family-capital sorting that continues among whatever reduced capacity remains, an omission the proposal itself acknowledges as a risk
